pattern: With at most 4 targets there are only 16 subsets to worry about, so a bitmask knapsack over nums (each number bumped up to the LCM of whichever subset of targets it will cover) finds the cheapest way to cover every target at least once.
hints: One increased number can satisfy several targets at once, as long as it becomes a common multiple of all of them; the cheapest way to make a number a multiple of a value L is to round it up to the next multiple of L.
Since target has at most 4 entries, there are only 15 non-empty subsets of targets; precompute the LCM of every subset once.

Do a bitmask DP over which targets are already covered: for each number in nums, in turn, try assigning it to cover any one subset (paying the rounding-up cost for that subset's LCM) or leaving it untouched, and keep the cheapest way to reach the full covered mask.

edge_inputs.description: statement example 2, one number covers two targets
single num, single target, maximum single-element gap
four targets, target length equals nums length exactly
covering two targets together costs more than covering them with two separate numbers
one already-satisfied target must not block covering the rest cheaply

stress.description: random nums with 1 to 4 random targets
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 20000)))
    m = rng.randint(1, min(4, n))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    target = [rng.randint(1, 10000) for _ in range(m)]
    return [nums, target]
